concepts.title: Baye's Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Baye's Theorem

We again consider the conditional probability statement:

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\mid \text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\ldots +\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)}$

in which we have used the theorem of Total Probability to replace $\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$. Now

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{A} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid \text{A} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( \text{A} \right)$

Substituting this in the expression for $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\mid \text{B} \right)$ we immediately obtain the result

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\mid \text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid \text{A} \right)\times \text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\ldots +\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)}$

This is true for any event $\text{A}$ and so, replacing $\text{A}$ by ${{\text{A}}_{\text{i}}}$ gives the result, known as Bayes' theorem as

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{i}}}\mid \text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{\text{i}}} \right)\times \text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{i}}} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\ldots +\text{P}\left( \text{B}\mid {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)}$

This theorem is used in the cases where the required event has already taken place and you are required to find the probability under some previous stage conditions. For example,

The required event A has occurred then find the probability that it occurred through event $\text{B}$ (which is defined in prior stages)

Illustration

In a test an examine either guesses or copies of knows the answer to a multiple choice question with four choices. The probability that he make a guess is 1/3 and the probability that he copies the answer is 1/ 6. The probability that his answer is correct given that he copied it, is 1/8. Find the probability that he knew the answer to the question given that he correctly answered it. [I.I.T. 1991] 

Solution: 

First of all, to find out whether this question is of Bayes’ theorem or not.

See the language of the question, "he correctly answered it" i.e. the event has already occurred. And we asked to find the probability of occurred event on some prior event i.e. "he knows the answer".

Let us define different events so as to ease out the question.

${{\text{A}}_{1}}\equiv $ the examinee guesses the answer

${{\text{A}}_{2}}\equiv $ the examine copies the answer

${{\text{A}}_{3}}\equiv $ the examinee knows the answer

$\text{A}\equiv $ the examinee answers correctly

Now according to question

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)=\frac{1}{3} \\ {} & \text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)=\frac{1}{6} \\ \end{array}$

Now notice that ${{A}_{1}}{{A}_{2}}{{A}_{3}}$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive event.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)=1$

we needed this to find the value of $\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)$

$\Rightarrow $ $\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}+\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)$ $~=1$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)$ $~=\frac{1}{2}$

We need to find the probability of $\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}}/\text{A} \right)$ which is equal to

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}}/\text{A} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)}$ … (i)

 

Ifor this we need the values of $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)$ seeing this from tree diagram we are given with $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)$ i.e. he answers correctly after copying

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)=\frac{1}{8}$

Now coming to $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)$

it is equal to $\frac{1}{4}$

as there are only 4 options to guess from and one option will be correct.

for $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)$, it is equal to 1 , obviously because he knows the correct answer which can be known in 1 possible way only.

Now substituting all these values in (i)

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}}/\text{A} \right)$ $~=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)}$

$~=\frac{1\cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{4}\cdot \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{8}\cdot \frac{1}{6}+1\cdot \frac{1}{2}}$

$~=\frac{1}{2}\times \frac{48}{29}=\frac{24}{29}$

Illustration 

Three urns contain 6 red, 4 black, 4 red, 6 black and 5 red and 5 black balls respectively. One of the urns is selected at random and a ball is drawn from it. If the ball drawn is red, find the probability that is drawn from the first urn. 

Solution: 

Let ${{\text{E}}_{1}},{{\text{E}}_{2}},{{\text{E}}_{3}}$ and $\text{A}$ be the events defined as follows:

${{\text{E}}_{1}}=$ urn first is chosen, ${{\text{E}}_{2}}=$ urn second is chosen,

${{\text{E}}_{3}}=$ urn third is chosen, and $\text{A}=$ ball drawn is red.

Since there are three urns and one of the three urns is chosen at random, therefore

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{2}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{3}} \right)=\frac{1}{3}$

If ${{\text{E}}_{1}}$ has already occurred, then urn first has been chosen which contains 6 red and 4 black balls. The probability of drawing a red ball from it is 6/10.

So, $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)=\frac{6}{10}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Similarly }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{2}} \right)=\frac{4}{10}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{3}} \right)=\frac{5}{10}$

We are required to find $\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{1}}/\text{A} \right)$, i.e. given that the ball drawn is red, what is the probability that it is drawn from the first urn.

By Bayes rule

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{1}}/\text{A} \right)=$ $\frac{\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)}{\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{2}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{2}} \right)+\text{P}\left( {{\text{E}}_{3}} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{A}/{{\text{E}}_{3}} \right)}$

$~=\frac{\frac{1}{3}\times \frac{6}{10}}{\frac{1}{3}\times \frac{6}{10}+\frac{1}{3}\times \frac{4}{10}+\frac{1}{3}\times \frac{5}{10}}=\frac{6}{15}=\frac{2}{5}$
